context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m working on the philosophy question about when it’s okay to kill 1 to save 5, and what act consequentialism says. i kinda get the basic idea, but I’m confused about how it applies to the trolley/Fuel/Blood cases.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—start from the core test. For an act consequentialist, what matters most in judging a particular action in a particular case?

Now apply that to each case: in Trolley, Fuel, and Blood, what are the options, and how many deaths result from each option?

Once you lay that out, ask yourself: would act consequentialism treat those cases differently at all, or the same? Why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the response brief and use guided Socratic questions rather than explain the full view directly. Since the student already has the basic idea, I should push them to apply that idea to the specific cases in the assignment. I will focus tightly on the act consequentialist framework and the listed cases, and ask questions that move toward comparing outcomes, then toward evaluating whether that framework seems plausible. I will not provide a solution or writing for the student.

exchanges.student: I think it mostly cares about the consequences of that specific act, like which option leads to fewer deaths. So rn I’m guessing it treats them basically the same if the numbers are 1 dies vs 5 die, but I’m not fully sure how Blood fits.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the key pressure point. In Blood, what exactly does the agent do to the one person, and what happens to the five if they don’t?

Then ask: if the outcome totals are the same, does act consequentialism have any built-in reason to care about the difference between redirecting harm and using someone’s body/resources?

Try stating Blood in that format.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's useful starting point but avoid confirming a full answer. I will direct them to distinguish the structure of the cases by identifying what the agent does in Blood versus the others. This keeps the exchange Socratic, bite-sized, and focused on the assignment's central comparison. The question will move them toward seeing whether act consequentialism is sensitive only to outcomes or also to the means.

exchanges.student: ok, I think in Blood the one person gets killed for their blood/organs or something, and if u don’t do it then the five die. So if it’s just outcome totals, then act consequentialism prob wouldn’t care about that difference, which feels kinda wrong tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. So you’ve identified the likely conflict: same numbers, different moral verdicts.

Which pair seems most important for showing that problem—Trolley vs Blood, or Fuel vs Blood? What morally relevant difference are you tempted to point to?

Don’t name a theory yet; just say what feature seems to make killing the one more objectionable in Blood.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's progress and push them to articulate the objection in philosophical terms without supplying the doctrine. The next productive step is to compare cases that act consequentialism treats alike but intuition may separate, then ask the student what principle could explain that separation. This advances toward the assignment's final part about the 'right general account' while keeping the response concise and non-directive.

exchanges.student: I’d prob use Trolley vs Blood. The difference that jumps out is that in Blood the one person is being used as a means, not just harmed as a side effect, I think.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong direction. Now test it: in Fuel, is the one person merely a side effect, or are they being used to save the five?

If your distinction handles Trolley and Blood, does it also sort Fuel the way your intuitions do?

Try classifying all three cases using that one contrast.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will reinforce the student's promising distinction and guide them to test it against the remaining case, Fuel, so they can see whether their emerging principle has enough explanatory power. I will not give them a doctrine or phrasing to use; instead I will ask compact questions that move directly toward the assignment's request for a general account and for evaluation of act consequentialism.

exchanges.student: uhh I’m a little stuck on Fuel rn. I think maybe Fuel also uses the person kinda instrumentally, which would make it closer to Blood than Trolley?
exchanges.tutor:
Slow down and separate two questions: in Fuel, is the person’s death part of what makes the rescue work, or would the rescue work even if that person somehow didn’t die?

In other words, is the death a tool in the plan, or a byproduct of carrying it out?

Which way does Fuel look to you under that test?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on a specific case, so I should give minimal structure without resolving it for them. I will use guided questions that isolate the morally relevant feature: whether the person's death is part of the plan or merely a foreseen byproduct. This keeps to the assignment, remains Socratic, and avoids giving the answer directly.

exchanges.student: ok yeah, under that test Fuel seems more like a byproduct, not the tool itself. so maybe Trolley and Fuel go together, and Blood is the different one.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now connect the pieces.

If act consequentialism focuses only on total outcomes, what would it say about Trolley, Fuel, and Blood?

And if your intuition separates Blood from the other two, what does that suggest act consequentialism is missing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an important distinction. I should now guide them to connect that distinction back to act consequentialism and then to the assignment’s final evaluative question about the right general account. I will keep it brief, ask them to articulate the implication, and avoid formulating the final thesis for them.

exchanges.student: I think act consequentialism would say all 3 are okay if it’s 1 death instead of 5. If Blood still seems worse, then it’s missing the difference between killing as a means and killing as a side effect.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s the core critique taking shape.

Now be careful: is your emerging view saying it’s sometimes okay to kill one to save five only when the one is not used as a means?

Or do you think that still needs refinement? Try to state the rule in one or two plain sentences, then test it against the three cases.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will acknowledge the student's progress and push them one step further toward the assignment's requirement of a 'right general account.' I should avoid naming or supplying a full theory, and instead ask them to propose a limited principle and then test its limits. This keeps the exchange concise, Socratic, and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: yeah, I think my rule is: it can be okay to kill 1 to save 5 when the death is a side effect of stopping a bigger harm, but not when the person is used as a means to save the 5. That would make Trolley and Fuel maybe okay, but Blood not okay.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a solid draft. Now test its strength: does your rule explain why the numbers alone don’t settle the case?

And one more check: are you saying the moral difference comes from outcomes, or from how the one person figures in the agent’s plan?

Answer those, and you’ll have the backbone of your response.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a promising tentative principle. My next step is to stress-test it rather than confirm it as final, because the rules require guided discovery and no direct completion of the assignment. I will ask a focused follow-up that helps them evaluate whether their rule is truly a general account and whether it answers the act consequentialist part of the prompt. This keeps the response bite-sized and on-theme.

exchanges.student: yeah, it explains why just counting deaths isn’t enough. I’m saying the difference comes from how the person figures in the plan, not just the outcome totals.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. You now have the main claim.

Next, can you lay out your response in 4 parts: what act consequentialism says, how it handles the 3 cases, why that seems mistaken, and your better general rule?

Write those as short bullet points yourself. If you want, send your bullets and I’ll critique the reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a workable core position. I should not provide a full answer or write the response for them, so I will push them to assemble their own argument structure in concise steps. This follows the Socratic method by turning their conclusion into a self-generated outline: state act consequentialism, apply it to cases, criticize it, and present their alternative principle. I will keep it brief and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student:
ok, here’s my try:

- Act consequentialism judges each act by its consequences, so saving 5 at the cost of 1 looks right.
- So it would treat Trolley, Fuel, and Blood basically the same if the result is fewer deaths overall.
- That seems mistaken bc Blood feels worse than Trolley/Fuel, since the 1 person is used as a means there.
- A better rule is that it’s only okay when the 1 person’s death is a side effect, not when they’re used instrumentally.
exchanges.tutor:
This is a good backbone. The weak spot is the last two bullets: you assert the distinction, but why should it matter morally?

Ask yourself: what is objectionable about using a person as a means that is not present in mere side-effect cases? And does your rule say “always wrong,” or just “not justified by numbers alone”?

Tighten that reasoning, and your outline becomes much stronger.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a concise outline. I should evaluate its strengths and push for clarification where reasoning is still compressed, without rewriting it or supplying polished language. To stay Socratic and bite-sized, I will identify the main philosophical pressure point—justifying why the means/side-effect distinction matters—and ask one or two targeted questions that help the student deepen the argument on their own.
